data.title: Wipro: Strong Financials But Lacks Alpha Amid Sectoral Headwinds
data.description:
The recent correction in Wipro (NYSE:WIT) may provide a favorable short-term trade entry point, but looking at medium-term prospects fundamentally, I am not very bullish. The fourth largest IT services and consultancy firm in India is likely to be hit by decreased discretionary spending over the next few quarters. There are no direct tariff impacts on exports from the IT sector in India, but overall economic sluggishness threatens to flatten demand.

Income Oriented Investments can be Directed Elsewhere too

The strong free cash flow of the Indian IT sector, in general, is true for Wipro as well. Looking at the free cash flow numbers for Infosys (INFY) and Wipro, both show strong numbers relative to its revenue size and consistently so for years. In fact, free cash flow per share for INFY is far superior at .98 (as per the last reported Indian Annual financial year ending Mar’24) compared to Wipro’s 0.19.

Income-focused investors may have seen a strong dividend and buyback history for WIT. However, Infosys has a higher trailing twelve-month dividend yield of 3.5% vs 2.48% for Wipro. Wipro’s Indian competitors are equally strongly placed, if not better placed, for buybacks and have a good history of the same. If looking for income, there are other Indian IT exposures that could pay off equally well, if not better.

Valuation Comparison with INFY

While the forward PE ratio for INFY is at 21.85, Wipro trades at a forward PE ratio of 19.55. There is almost nothing to choose between the two. However, the 5-year average forward PE ratio for INFY is 26.54, while that for WIT is 22.57. We have a greater valuation discount for a market leader like INFY so far compared to WIT. Overall, although in comparison to INFY, WIT looks like a weaker case, WIT in itself has no red flags and is a sound investment material if the outlook is good. The comparison on financials is nitpicking at best among two stable, financially sound number sets. The PE discount for INFY is a reflection of how the share prices have moved in the past 3–6 months.

Decently Poised to Grow but Management Churn

Wipro acquired Capco in 2021 to strengthen its focus on banking, wealth management, and insurance. This was a transformational move aimed at getting a stronghold on the sector in terms of technology services and consulting.

Long-term tailwinds from transformational activities that benefit the IT sector should benefit Wipro also, especially in areas like cybersecurity, cloud services, data analytics, and AI. Wipro is prepared to take advantage of this growth with strategic investments and partnerships with Azure and AWS, among other technology partners. However, is there any special case for Wipro against other competitors in the Indian IT services segment? I don’t see any Standout theme for Wipro showing an alpha from the baseline sectoral gains, when it comes to GenAI or futuristic exponential growth in the IT world. The case is further dampened considering the valuation benefits of choosing WIT against a traditionally strong counter like INFY.

Over the past few years, there have also been proactive strategic business structure realignment and leadership changes to focus on agility and growth. Wipro moved away from its only geography-based structure to a business line approach in defining business units. All changes were aimed to change the focus on broader transformation objectives than the earlier project-based approach. However, the changed cultural approach has not seen a great impact on gross profit or revenues outside of market-wide gains in Indian IT.

I am not very convinced about the lack of stability strategy at the top and view the management changes as more a transition risk area than a proactive improvement – growth numbers back that feel. In mid-2020, when Thierry Delaporte took over the reins of the company, there was a lot of hope for significant growth. That has not really translated (barring how the industry gained post-2020 anyway). More recently, Wipro has gone back to local leadership, with Srinivas Pallia taking over as CEO. Constant changes instead of meaningful time spent on a strategy remain a concern for Wipro.

Imminent Order Book Issues

In Q4 2024, Wipro closed 17 deals totaling $1b. This is ballpark a similar number for the past 3 quarters. However, lower contract signings, drop-offs, and future order book tapering look like imminent threats as discretionary spending takes a hit in developed markets. Management guidance is conservative and not too far out from the usual +1 to -1% revenue growth in the current quarter.

With order-book drags impacting the topline, Wipro is likely to continue focusing and improving its operational efficiency (which resulted in a 3-year-high operating margin of 17.5% in Q4 2024). A continued growth here could be a signal of improving investor sentiment.

Hold Wipro and Enter on Corrections

Overall, Wipro’s financials are robust, and improving operational efficiency is noticeable. However, in terms of outperforming Indian IT leaders like INFY, it has not been able to win bids and large deals as much as it should, to improve its investment thesis. Valuation discounts are also not in play to merit a fresh Buy at this point. As the outlook for the entire segment is cloudy at the moment due to macroeconomic headwinds, the order book could take a hit in the upcoming quarters. Strategic directional clarity is also a concern as the messaging from leadership changes reeks of proactive intent but lacks a consistent directional theme.

Existing investors can continue to hold WIT for the longer haul, although moving money away to INFY serves a better purpose as of today’s visibility, both in terms of capital growth and income from dividends and buybacks. Fresh investors should consider INFY or better still await for clarity on the macroeconomic environment or further corrections in WIT before entering afresh.

data.title: Snowline Gold: Explorations And Bullish Gold Hoist Shares (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A "Buy" rating for Shares of Snowline Gold Corp.

With this article, we consider the possibility of a higher "Buy" rating for the OTCQB-listed shares of Snowline Gold Corp. (OTCQB:SNWGF), which corresponds to an upgrade of the "Hold" rating given in the previous analysis dated September 27, 2024.

Hereinafter, this company is referred to simply as the "company" or "Snowline" or "SNWGF."

The upgrade follows a record rally in the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR). The current price of US$ 3,012.32/ounce represents an increase of 14.81% year-to-date and 28.18% year-on-year, driven by strong demand for safe-haven assets amid geopolitical tensions and recession risks exacerbated by the tariff war. The extraordinary bullish sentiment regarding the gold price is generating significant market interest in the Valley deposit of the Rogue Project in the Yukon Territory, Snowline's flagship asset in its portfolio.

The Rogue Gold Project consists of 4,580 mineral claims covering approximately 94,397 hectares in the Selwyn Basin of the Yukon Territory. The Rogue Gold Project is not the only property in Snowline's portfolio that

"sits within the prolific Tintina Gold Province, host to multiple million-ounce-plus gold mines and deposits across the central Yukon and Alaska.", the company says,

as Snowline is also exploring the Einarson property with its major gold discoveries at Jupiter and the Cynthia property. Our previous articles provide more information on the assets briefly touched upon here, as we are now focusing on developments that have occurred since our last analysis and which mainly refer to the Valley deposit of the Rogue gold project.

Since Last "Hold" Rating: How Snowline Gold Corp. Performed

In the previous article, we predicted that the market would appreciate the exploration results from the Rogue property in particular, as they reveal amazing potential for gold mineralization at the company's flagship project. These drilling results come at a time when interest in gold has become a hot topic, with the yellow metal dominating the headlines of financial news platforms due to its unprecedented rise.

As expected, a combination of promising drilling and bullish gold prices helped Snowline's shares post a positive change of 23.67%, significantly outperforming the S&P 500's negative change of 11.60%. Returns are from our last article and at the time of writing. In line with our previous analysis, it made sense to hold Snowline stock in the portfolio, but the previous "Hold" rating also contained the signal to take advantage of any significant decline in the share price to potentially strengthen the position ahead of the uptrend. This dip in the stock price of Snowline occurred around November 21, 2024, " following a steep weekly fall" in the price of gold a few days earlier. At that time, it was widely believed that the Federal Reserve was about to become more cautious in its interest rate cuts to counter inflation concerns triggered by uncertainty about increased tariffs and other promises made by newly elected US President Trump to stimulate the US economy. Due to what we call a "healthy" temporary pullback in gold prices, Snowline shares offered a more favorable entry point for pre-rally stock purchases, and investors who adopted the investment style without neglecting any aspect of our previous recommendation could look forward to even stronger results.

Gold Price Outlook: A Fertile Context Where Snowline's Market Cap Can Flourish

In another article about another gold company, we reported on the gold price forecasts of relevant US analysts. We wrote:

These factors prompted UBS and Macquarie to raise their price forecasts earlier this month to $3,200/ounce and $3,500/ounce, respectively. Last week, Goldman Sachs and Bank of America raised their gold price forecasts to US$ 3,300/ounce (from US$ 3,100/ounce) by 2025 and US$ 3,500/ounce within the next two years, respectively.

Valley Deposit on Track for MRE Update and Preliminary Economic Assessment; But Also, Positive News from the Einarson Project and the Cynthia Property

We believe the following information will be useful to readers, including the map, as it provides context for discussing the company's analytical results, which, subsequent to the previous article, stemmed from the 2024 drill campaign:

"The Snowline Gold portfolio explores gold mineralization on an extensive land package of over 360,000 hectares in the Selwyn Basin in Canada's Yukon Territory. There are more than 30 gold targets in eight projects, reflecting the importance of the district in terms of precious metals' presence. Snowline Gold is focused on the Rogue, Einarson and Cynthia projects," as we wrote in our previous article.

The Valley Deposit on the Rogue Project

Since our last article, the most significant updates have been regarding the Valleys deposit, the Rogue project, which is on track for its MRE update and preliminary economic assessment, and, to a lesser extent, the Einarson project and the Cynthia property.

As for the Valley on the Rogue Project, this is a gold deposit that could be even richer, cheaper (and therefore more profitable) to exploit, as well as larger than expected. This is because the analytical results from the 2024 drilling campaign indicate progressive and significantly improved mineralization in three main areas of interest:

A) "High-grade."

B) "Non-refractory gold mineralization" (i.e., cyanide can be used, resulting in a more cost-effective gold recovery process).

C) "Mineralization is commencing at the surface or near-surface."

This is evident not only in the innermost, continental parts of the Rogue property, i.e., more in the core target areas of the Valley deposit and its northwestern part towards the northeast side of the Valley deposit, but also in the more peripheral parts of the gold resource area at the " southwestern edge" and " northwestern end" of the Valley deposit as well as "northeast and southeast edges" of the Valley deposit.

Scott Berdahl, CEO and Director of Snowline, commented on the margins of the Valley deposit, highlighting a feature that places this deposit several notches above many other gold systems of the same type:

" […] They consistently deliver intervals spanning hundreds of metres of continuous mineralization, generally above one gram per tonne gold and in cases above two grams per tonne-results in themselves that would be singular highlights in many other systems of this type. We are pleased to see this consistency around what are currently the edges of Valley, and we look forward to incorporating these holes into an updated mineral resource estimate along with 20 additional holes from our 2024 drill campaign at Valley still to come, as well as regional drilling results." "

The analytical results, most of which have yet to be reviewed and published, offer tremendous upside potential for the share price as they continue to be promising, along with the rising gold price. Starting from the southeast of the existing MREs, they represent an incremental improvement over the model used to estimate the known MREs mentioned above, thereby progressively de-risking the project.

Plans for the future

Scott Berdahl, CEO and Director of Snowline, said:

" We are excited to continue our rapid advancement of Valley with an updated MRE along with a PEA to quantify the compound effects of these positive attributes on potential future economics."We are also looking forward to another big season in 2025, with five drills currently on site and ready to commence a program of district-scale exploration alongside advancement and potential expansion of Valley, commencing in mid-May. The details of this program will be outlined in a future release." "

The Jupiter Target on the Einarson Project and the Cynthia Property

Also, the company continues to demonstrate its strong presence in an area with regional potential. The Jupiter target area (approximately 30 km north of the Valley deposit) of the Einarson Project (adjacent to the Rogue Project) hosts additional zones of striking mineralization.

According to the company, this deepens the understanding of a "secondary east-west structural control on mineralization." These results reinforce the company's belief that significant gold deposits can be discovered, as the area remains unexplored.

Regarding the Cynthia project, supported by the silver anomaly discovered there, Scott Berdahl said in November of last year:

" "We are further encouraged by ongoing indications of widespread regional gold fertility across our extensive mineral tenure in the eastern Yukon, evidenced by the advancement of yet another greenfield target, on our Cynthia Project, in the broader Rogue Plutonic Complex." "

Funding for Exploration and Other Advances

The company is funding exploration with US$30.3 million in cash and cash equivalents and is debt-free as of December 30, 2024. The company reported operating cash outflows of US$23.4 million and capital expenditures of US$3.0 million over the prior 12 months through the December 2024 quarter.

Snowline Gold is targeting gross proceeds of CA$20.02 million (approximately US$14.1 million as of this writing) from the private placement of flow-through shares announced earlier this month.

" "This financing will serve to top up an already strong treasury for Snowline, allowing for efficient use of exploration capital and providing a long runway to continue our exploration this year and next as we work to unlock a district," said Scott Berdahl, CEO & Director of Snowline. "

The Stock Price: Buying Now Is Justified, But It Is Always Advisable To Wait For A Dip Amid Volatility

At the time of this writing, Snowline Gold Corp. shares listed on the OTCQB under the symbol SNWGF were trading at a price of US$ 5.19 apiece, representing a market capitalization of US$779.12 million.

Investors may want to wait for a price dip before adding to their positions. This is likely to happen if the share price falls below the MA band and when it compares more favorably than it currently does as follows: the current price of $5.19/share is above the middle point of US$4.44/share of the 52-week range of US$2.70 per share to $6.18 per share.

The 14-day Relative Strength Indicator could serve as a benchmark. If it falls from the current 46.21 to the oversold level of 20, the stock price could signal a true dip. This occurred in late November, amid growing fears that the Fed could keep interest rates "higher for longer" due to inflation concerns related to the "Trump trade."

While the intensified tariff war may be fueling inflation concerns, market participants appear more concerned about the consequences for global growth, including a significant slowdown in the US economic cycle. As a safe haven against the negative effects of a recession or downturn, the price of gold will rise, not fall, which will also ignite upward pressure on the Snowline stock price. Following this logic, it is not expected that the increased tariffs will lead to a dip in Snowline's share price. In addition, interest rate traders now predict the US Federal Reserve will cut the key interest rate five times by 25 basis points each by the end of 2025. This gives cause for optimism, as it makes zero-income-bearing gold bars more attractive than US Treasury bonds.

There are potential events that, according to analysts at major US banks,

"could spark selling and create better entry points for investors," Yahoo Finance reports, citing these as the most likely: A) "a Russia-Ukraine peace deal" or "a potential sharp equity sell-off", according to Goldman commodities strategist Lina Thomas.

In our view, geopolitical tensions are more likely to flare up than be resolved peacefully. Moreover, with the global trade war in full swing, the climate is highly uncertain, and it is impossible to assess the circumstances to estimate the likelihood of a ceasefire.

Another major stock market sell-off is much easier to predict, with analysts at Trading Economics predicting (see the "forecast" section of this website) that the US500 index, currently at 5,067.46 points, will fall to 4,784.84 points by the end of this quarter. This stock market sell-off could push Snowline shares towards a plunge, continuing the work done halfway by the three-day losing streak marked by increased volatility in early April.

However, the negative impact on Snowline stock is likely to be less than expected, as the 24-month beta is 0.64. This suggests that Snowline stock is less susceptible to panic attacks than the average U.S.-listed stock.

Given the consistent exploration results and record-high gold price sentiment, the growth prospects are promising. Should investors decide to purchase Snowline Gold Corp. shares at this price level, which is also significantly below the peak reached in early April, this would not be an unreasonable move, in our view.

Conclusion

Snowline Gold Corp. is a Canadian gold explorer operating three promising projects in the Selwyn District of the Yukon Territory. The Valley gold deposit, the Rogue Project, is Snowline's flagship project. Valley has made the most progress from greenfield to potential multi-million-ounce production in just a few years (from September 2021).

Assay results reveal impressive mineralization characterized by three distinct characteristics: 1) high-grade mineralization. 2) refractory gold ore, which makes gold recovery methods more advantageous. 3) mineralization that begins at or near the surface. This mineralization is continuously evolving, regardless of whether drill results are from the core of the deposit or its edge.

The company believes in the existence of high-yielding gold resources at a regional level, as demonstrated by drilling on the Einarson and Cynthia concessions, the former of which is located in close proximity to the Rogue project.

The outlook for gold prices is positive, further enhancing the value of the shares for shareholders. Investors should take advantage of price dips to strengthen their positions in Snowline Gold Corp. stock. Dips are possible. It would not be unreasonable to buy shares at these share price levels, which are well below the peak reached in early April, given the positive outlook supported by consistent explorations and positive sentiment in the gold market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How Commodities Can Help Diversify A Portfolio During Market Volatility
data.description:
As the U.S. announces a 90-day pause on most tariffs, except for those involving China, the ongoing trade uncertainty has been driving market volatility. And some investors are looking to gold and other commodities to diversify their portfolio. Hussein Allidina, Managing Director and Head of Commodities, TD Asset Management joins MoneyTalk to discuss his outlook for gold and oil as well as the potential role of commodities in a balanced portfolio.

Transcripts

Greg Bonnell: Markets continue to be volatile amid all this trade uncertainty. But for investors who are looking to diversify their portfolios, what role can commodities play in that portfolio? Joining us now to discuss, Hussein Allidina, Managing Director and Head of Commodities with TD Asset Management. Hussein, always great to have you on the program.

Hussein Allidina: Thanks for having me.

Greg Bonnell: These are very interesting times, if I can put it that way. And it can be stressful, too, for investors watching these markets. Let's talk about commodities and the role that they can play in all this.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so I think, over the course of probably the last 20 years, the need for diversification in a portfolio was tempered by the fact that equities and fixed income afforded that negative correlation. I think this year, late last year, arguably into the future, we're going to need more diversification in our portfolios. Commodities have definitely had a challenging April, to be clear.

But year to date, Benchmark Commodities, the Bloomberg Commodity Index is flat, maybe down 1% compared to equities that are off 17. And that has largely to do with the fact that commodities are coincident indicators. They reflect the supply and demand, or attempt to reflect, the supply and demand today, whereas equities and fixed income are anticipatory.

Again, for the better part of the 20 years until COVID, you didn't, maybe, need diversification because inflation was well-behaved, allowing for that negative correlation. The reason that investors have, and continue to seek, commodity derivatives in their portfolios is largely for that negative correlation that they afford to equities and fixed income, and importantly, the positive beta, or the positive relationship, that they exhibit with inflation. If we're moving into an environment where inflation is going to be more elevated, more volatile, commodities are one of the things that hedge against that volatility.

Greg Bonnell: Now, obviously, we've seen a lot of volatility. And that has led to bear markets across indices that were hitting record highs only a couple of months ago. Balanced portfolios, if we're talking this space, what do they perform like historically coming out of a market sell-off like we've had?

Hussein Allidina: So, if you look historically at what the optimal allocation to commodities should be, through time, the math will suggest an allocation of between 4% and 10%. If you look at a portfolio that has commodities-- so commodities by themselves afford equity-like returns but higher volatility. So looking at it in isolation, I'd rather it be long equities. If I'm picking one asset, I'd rather it be long equities versus commodities because the Sharpe on equities through time is higher.

But because commodities afford that negative correlation, when you add that into your portfolio, you're improving the efficient frontier or you're getting better return for any given level of risk. If you look over the course of the last 30, 40 years, a 60/40 portfolio that has 10% commodities has a shallower drawdown. And that's important, I think, in today's environment. From 1970 to present, a 60/40 portfolio with a 10% allocation to a passive commodity index has resulted in a shallower drawdown 66% of the time. So you're able to temper the damage in environments where things get challenged.

Greg Bonnell: When I take a look at the commodity-- I mean, the commodity space, once you start to dig in, you could be talking about how gold's been reacting.

Hussein Allidina: Yep.

Greg Bonnell: Oil is reacting in a different way, along with some of the other metals. But behind all this, of course, once we get through the noise, is about supply out there of these key commodities. You take a look at the tight supply space that we're in right now, I think, is the argument you're going to make. What does it all mean?

Hussein Allidina: Well, I think it's important. Over the course of the better part over the last 10, 12 years, commodity prices have been challenged. That's a very normal part of the commodity cycle, where we're exploiting the investment that we made in the early 2000s. Today, what the market is debating, or trying to get a handle on, is how challenged demand is going to be on the heels of the tariff tensions that are hopefully at a peak.

The supply side matters, Greg. But if there's no demand-- and we've joked about this. There's no whale oil supply. But there's also no whale oil demand, so it doesn't matter. Right? Today, the market is trying to discount how challenged GDP is going to be, and whether it's going to be challenged enough to have the supply side act as a constraint. When we look at commodities like oil, you're seeing concerns on the demand side because of the tariffs, at the same time that OPEC is adding more barrels than expected back to the market. And that's why--

Greg Bonnell: What's that all about? I wanted to talk to you that day. What was that all about?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so I think it's a couple of things. And I don't know the answer. If I look at the fundamentals, year-to-date, inventories have been tighter than the market expected. So if I'm giving you the bullish pitch, it's like, look OPEC is bringing barrels back to the market because the market needs it. And you see that in the backwardation that we've seen in WTI and Brent, et cetera. And that tighter balance is a function of demand being a little bit stronger than expected, and supply, particularly out of Venezuela, disappointing on the heels of sanctions.

There's also an argument that OPEC producers outside of Saudi, Kuwait, UAE have not complied with their quotas. So is this a way for the core three OPEC members to punish the rest of OPEC? Maybe there's an element of that. There's also speculation that President Trump is going to be going to the region at the beginning of May. So maybe there's some appeasement happening in advance of that.

And then lastly, which is not being talked about a ton, you are seeing a larger military presence that the US is exhibiting in the Indian Ocean, with a number of their stealth bombers now parked there, flying distance from Iran. Unclear if there is some additional barrels being added in anticipation of potentially less production. What the market is grappling with right now, I think, on the second derivative, supply has gotten more bearish than we thought four weeks ago. And on a second derivative basis, demand has clearly gotten more challenged.

Is demand going to grow by 300,000 barrels a day or a million barrels a day? It's a big unknown. But the volatility and the weakness in price is, I think, sowing some challenge as we look into the future. We're not going to see the production growth that we were expecting, or that President Trump was expecting, out of the US if oil prices are at $50-55 a barrel.

Greg Bonnell: I took a detour there because I was personally interested. I got to get back on track. I was supposed to ask you about gold in this run that we've seen. Quite positive.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, and we've been constructive gold. And we like gold because of the merits it affords in portfolio construction. So you see today that there is a flight to safety and gold is benefiting. We like gold because when we look at what emerging market central banks are doing-- we've talked about this. They've been buying gold in a pretty robust manner for the better part of the last 12 to 16 months. And notwithstanding the fact that they've been buying gold, their reserves, their bank reserves, are still predominantly north of 80%, 90% sitting in fiat currency, sitting in the US dollar, sitting in the euro.

If I was a wealth manager for the central bank, I'd say you probably want to have some diversification in your assets. When we talk to our clients, we're talking to them about the importance of portfolio construction and diversification. You're a central bank, you're the Chinese central bank, the Indian central bank, and all your reserves, or almost all your reserves, are sitting in paper. And we've seen, over the course of the last 16 months, instances where the US government has confiscated reserves from Russia, from Afghanistan.

I don't know that that's the risk that you want to take. It's not necessarily de-dollarization. But on the margin, I think it's better portfolio construction.

Original Post

data.title: Elastic: Inexpensive But Better Opportunities Exist
data.description:
While Elastic's (NYSE:ESTC) Search business is a growth driver at the moment, and has helped renew investor interest in Elastic, Search is still a relatively small part of the business. Elastic's growth continues to grind lower, which could be a result of the company's weak competitive positioning within the larger Observability and Security segments. On a positive note, Elastic's margins continue to improve, and the company has a realistic chance of achieving GAAP profitability in FY26.

I previously suggested that the optimism created by the exit of Elastic's CFO and improving sales productivity may not be warranted. At the time, I felt that Elastic's modest growth and low services gross margins indicated ongoing weakness. While Elastic's share price is down by over 20% since then, this has been driven by a pullback in the broader market as much as anything.

Elastic's recent performance has been slightly stronger than I expected, but my view of the company remains unchanged. Elastic is a solid company that can provide reasonable returns at the right entry point. I don't see the company being a large winner though, and there are better opportunities on the market with similar or lower valuations.

Market Conditions

The growth tailwind generative AI is providing to Elastic's Search business is key to near-term returns. While I believe that Elastic is well positioned to capitalize on the opportunity, specialized vector databases could win out in the most demanding use cases.

Pinecone is a high-profile example of the vector database competition that Elastic faces. The company was launched in 2019 and reportedly generated around $27 million revenue in 2024, an increase of roughly 100% over 2023. Pinecone raised at a $750 million valuation in early 2023. While Elastic has given limited information about the success of its generative AI efforts, I would not be surprised if Pinecone's vector database revenue was similar to or greater than Elastic's. Elastic has suggested that it has the most downloaded vector database on the market though.

The number of job openings mentioning Elasticsearch in the job requirements doesn't point towards a surge in demand driven by generative AI either. This could be due to general weakness within SMBs at the moment though, and Elastic's shift in focus to expansion within larger organizations.

Elastic Business Updates

While Elastic hasn't given any hard numbers, its Search segment is the strongest part of the business at the moment, with growth accelerating in Q4. Around 40% of Elastic's business comes from observability, 25% from security and the remaining 35% from search. Search growth is unsurprisingly being driven by the adoption of RAG in LLM applications and agentic workflows could support growth going forward.

In Q3, Elastic signed five generative AI deals worth in excess of $1 million annually, up from three in the prior quarter. Over 1,750 customers are now using Elastic for generative AI use cases and more than 270 of those are spending in excess of $100,000 annually. This is up from 1,550 customers using Elastic for generative AI use cases at the end of the prior quarter.

Elastic released its Rerank model in tech preview in the third quarter. Reranking models are used to reorder initial search results and help to improve the relevance of search results while limiting cost and latency. This is a fairly hot area at the moment, with MongoDB (MDB) recently acquiring Voyage AI for its embedding and retrieval models. Total consideration was $220 million even though Voyage AI is currently generating minimal revenue. Elastic's recent commentary regarding AI and vector search appears more positive than MongoDB's, with MongoDB suggesting that AI tailwinds are limited at the moment and are expected to remain modest in FY26.

Elastic also continues to embed AI in its solutions, recently releasing its attack discovery solution, which leverages LLMs to help automate threat discovery. Early interest in this product has reportedly been strong. Scale and access to data are likely to become increasingly important in this area though, which I believe puts Elastic on the back foot.

Financial Analysis

Elastic's revenue increased 17% YoY in the third quarter to $382 million, with Elastic Cloud revenue up 26% to $180 million. Consumption was stronger than expected, driven by larger customers, although shortfalls in customer commitments earlier in the year are still weighing on growth.

Elastic expects $379-$381 million of revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 13% YoY. While this guidance appears weak, Elastic expects modest foreign exchange headwinds and there are fewer days in the quarter relative to the prior year. I previously suggested that Elastic's guidance for Q3 and Q4 was extremely conservative. Despite this, I felt it was unlikely that growth would exceed 15% exiting the financial year. I now believe growth is likely to come in around 16%, although this is difficult to assess given current turbulence.

Elastic made field segmentation changes in its go-to-market motion earlier in the year, which has caused headwinds. The company also increased focus on larger organizations and high-potential mid-market customers, contributing to the disruption. Elastic has suggested that it is now moving past some of these headwinds though, and that it is beginning to realize benefits from the segmentation changes. This is difficult to see in the numbers at the moment, though. Growth of Elastic's customer base continues to moderate, particularly amongst smaller organizations. As a result, it will be difficult for Elastic to reaccelerate revenue growth, unless its expansion rate improves significantly. There is little reason to be positive on this front so far, as Elastic's net expansion rate has stabilized at 112%.

Profitability is one of the strongest aspects of Elastic's business at the moment, although I would argue that the company has pulled back on growth investments in order to hasten its path to profitability. Of note, Elastic's services gross margin continues to decline, which I believe is indicative of a soft demand environment, with underpriced services used as a customer acquisition tool.

Elastic's non-GAAP operating margin reached 17% in Q3, and the company is closing in on GAAP profitability. The company still isn't profitable on a GAAP basis, though. Over the past 12 months, Elastic's free cash flow margin has averaged around 17%. This positions Elastic to manage dilution from SBC and opportunistically increase repurchases when its share price is depressed.

Conclusion

While Elastic's share price has fallen in recent weeks, this appears to have been driven by a pullback in the broader market. Prior to that, investor sentiment towards the company had been improving on the back of stable relative growth and a sales force turnaround. It is hard to see the rebound in sales force productivity at the moment, although the change could be still working its way through Elastic's pipeline. Elastic is also closing in on GAAP profitability, and its cash flows are now fairly healthy. While Elastic's margins are improving, I expect growth to remain soft in the near term.

Elastic's revenue multiple is now near prior lows, which is suggestive of an attractive entry point. It should be kept in mind that Elastic's growth has moderated significantly over the past few years, though.

Compared to similar companies, Elastic also appears undervalued when adjusting for growth. While an argument can be made that Elastic's competitive positioning is weak, I think the market is overly pessimistic, particularly now that Elastic is beginning to generate strong cash flows, which can be used to offset dilution. Despite this, I believe that there are better opportunities available at the moment.

data.title: Unilever Is Back To Growth: Strategy, Numbers, And Potential
data.description:
Introduction

Unilever (NYSE:UL) has a powerful scale, a deep brand portfolio, and a geographical spread that allows the company to simultaneously maintain a strong position in Europe, increase sales in India, and develop new products in the premium segment of self-care. In 2024, the company showed organic sales growth of +4.2%, and the main contribution was made by sales volumes, rather than higher prices for goods. Over the past couple of years, everything has fallen on the company: covid, rising prices, strained relations with shareholders, the failure to buy GSK Consumer (GSK) for 50 billion pounds, and, finally, the complex transformation of the business structure from a heavy regional model to a new, more vibrant category. Former CEO Hein Schumacher launched the GAP 2030 strategy, which focuses on growth, focus on the strongest brands and management discipline. At the same time, the company announced the separation of the ice cream business into a separate structure — a logical decision, given its seasonality and different distribution model. Also, a wave of optimization took place: several thousand positions were reduced in order to focus on more marginal segments. Since March 2025, Fernando Fernandez, a former CFO and a man who knows the company well from the inside, has taken over the CEO post. His arrival is a signal to the market that Unilever is ready to implement its strategic goals at a high pace.

Business model

The company manages hundreds of brands, but since 2022 it has redesigned its architecture: instead of the old and sometimes archaic regional structure, everything is now organized by category. There are five business groups - Beauty & Wellbeing, Personal Care, Home Care, Foods and Ice Cream — and each has its own strategy, KPIs and its own area of responsibility for the result. This gave the portfolio balance: there is no such thing that one segment drags everything, if one has sunk then the others can compensate.

Beauty & Wellbeing is a growth area: there are premium stories, interesting deals like K18 and Nutrafol. Personal Care is Dove, and it relies on technology and efficiency. Home Care is about a mass segment where Unilever plays on the edge between accessibility and value upgrade. In food, things are more complicated: the market is overheated, especially in the United States, where even Hellmann's is losing share due to competition from local and more affordable brands. But due to Food Solutions and a focus on local tastes, the segment is holding up. But ice cream is generally a separate world: its own logistics, seasonality, models. That is why the company decided to separate Ice Cream into a separate business so that it would not interfere with the focus of others and become more flexible on its own.

If you look at the business model from above, you can see that Unilever has really restarted. Previously, everything was around regions, now it's around categories. The whole story is based on a powerful operating platform, from sustainable packaging to AI demand forecasting. Everything is being digitized, processes are being polished, and most importantly, the old hierarchy is really breaking down in the company.

GAP 2030 Development Strategy

In 2024, Unilever published its new GAP 2030 strategy, an ambitious relaunch of the entire system, from which brands remain in the portfolio to how people inside the company make decisions. The strategy focuses on a clear focus on the top 30 brands and 24 key markets that generate the company's main revenue. Everything else is on the way out, or at least in the "cutting the crutches and not interfering with the work of the main business" mode. To regain its leadership in the eyes of consumers, Unilever is seriously upgrading digital marketing, focusing on large, truly scalable innovations and boosting the premium segment.

An important element is internal transformation: scientific R&D platforms, AI in logistics and planning, rapid development of new products and increased operational speed on all fronts. At the same time, all KPIs are now tied to the implementation of GAP 2030, which finally creates the necessary link between strategy and reality. Already in the second half of 2024, the strategy began to bring results: the operating margin increased, and brands began to regain market share.

Competitors

In the food, hygiene and household segments, the company is fighting on two fronts at once: with global giants such as P&G (PG) and Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) and with an army of local niche brands that grow on flexibility, proximity to consumers and often simply due to lower prices. Everything is especially tough in the United States, where Unilever is constantly caught between two fires: it needs to keep margins, but at the same time it must not lose a buyer, and P&G has much more room to maneuver financially. In the beauty and wellness category, Unilever is still only catching up with the rest: the company is slowly building up its premium portfolio, and although it is still far from monsters like L'Oréal (OTCPK:LRLCY), gradual expansion is already underway. An important advantage of the company is emerging markets. Unilever has been operating well in India, Indonesia, and Brazil for a long time, and growth is still good there. At the same time, the threat from young, bold brands is growing — competition is only intensifying, commodity prices are starting to climb again, and regulators are tightening the screws.

And, of course, the story of the new tariffs in the United States in 2025 — imports are becoming more expensive, logistics are under pressure. But Unilever has a good buffer in this case: thanks to global production, it is less dependent on one region. On the contrary, new tariffs may hit cheaper competitors, opening up a window of opportunity for Unilever, especially where premium is playing and where locality is important.

Financials

In 2024, despite the conversion decrease in revenue in dollars (about $62.95 billion versus $65.80 billion in 2023), sales increased organically by +4.2%, and most importantly, almost entirely due to an increase in physical sales (+2.9%). Volumes grew in all five segments, especially in Beauty & Wellbeing (+5.1%) and Personal Care (+3.1%), which confirms the effectiveness of the focus on care and hygiene. Four consecutive quarters of growth of >2% is an excellent indicator that consumer interest is recovering.

Looking at the structure, the share of Beauty & Wellbeing has grown to 22%, and this is the fastest growing category (+6.5%), which is logical considering investments in premium brands. Personal Care and Foods each hold 22%, Home Care has sunk to 20%, and Ice Cream is stable (14%) — while the ice cream business itself has increased in absolute terms and reached €8.5 billion. This whole picture shows that Unilever is fine-tuning its strategy: growth is taking place even in a slow market, and the company is already ahead of many competitors in terms of dynamics. Management expects organic growth of +3-5% in 2025, although the first half of the year is likely to be sluggish. My model is a little more conservative, with a forecast of $64.37 billion (+2.3%). This is taking into account possible currency risks and the fact that M&A transactions are not expected in the near future. Nevertheless, the plan is quite working: the company is going to raise prices again in the second half of the year due to rising costs, while simultaneously supporting volume growth through marketing.

The year 2024 was a moment of profitability recovery for Unilever. Operating profit increased by 12.6%, the margin increased to 18.5%, and the gross margin generally reached the maximum of the decade — 45%. The company played well against the drop in raw material prices in the first half of the year, implemented a large-scale savings program (about €2.8 billion) and made the most of the volume growth. In segments where prices were even falling — for example, Home Care — by increasing sales and rationalizing the range, it was possible not only to maintain, but also to increase profits. As a result, EBITDA amounted to $12.72 billion with a margin of 20.2%, which almost returned the company to the pre-pandemic level.

By the end of 2024, net debt amounted to about $24 billion, and the Net Debt/EBITDA ratio dropped to a comfortable 2.0x, which is quite acceptable for a stable FMCG business. This reflects control over the structure: total debt remains at 72% of assets, as in previous years, and part of the debt has been reduced due to proceeds from the sale of the tea business and stable cash flow. With rising interest rates, debt servicing has become more expensive: interest costs in 2024 approached $1.15 billion, and the interest coverage indicator sank to 8.9x. At the same time, the debt structure is generally stable — mainly fixed-rate bonds, with an average rate of about 3%.

Unilever's capex, as for an FMCG company, remains moderate — in 2024, CapEx amounted to $1.80 billion (2.86% of revenue). Over the past four years, the volume of investments has grown steadily — by 70% in absolute terms, but this is understandable: inflation, capacity expansion in Asia and Africa, digital infrastructure, sustainable production. However, compared to the more capital—intensive P&G or Nestlé, Unilever's CapEx/Revenue is lower due to outsourcing, plant optimization, and a model where the brand is more important than the machine. For 2025, I plan to reduce Capex to $1.5 billion (2.3% of revenue), which is logical — the main construction projects are completed, the focus is shifting to efficiency and marketing. Investment starvation is not expected — even with a decrease, the company retains the ability to maintain infrastructure and launch new products. The Capex decline is likely temporary: Unilever is taking a break after an active investment phase, strengthening cash flow ahead of steps such as ice cream separation or new deals.

The company consistently generates high FCF. In 2024, the company earned about $8 billion. This was made possible by an increase in operating profit, zero change in working capital, and a relatively moderate level of investment. For 2025, I expect an FCF of $8.8 billion. CapEx is declining, and working with turnover gives an additional +$0.26 billion to the flow. As a result, the FCF margin can reach up to 12%, which will significantly strengthen financial flexibility. Even if the profit turns out to be slightly lower, the current structure of the business model, including the negative NWC cycle, will continue to support cache generation. Unilever uses this flow primarily for dividends, and then for moderate M&A or balance sheet strengthening. In 2025, expenses for the separation of Ice Cream are possible, but they most likely will not “eat up” FCF — on the contrary, the company may even receive an influx in the event of an IPO.

In 2024, the dividend yield was about 3.46%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $2 billion dollars for dividends, or a yield of 3.62%.

Valuation

The company's shares have grown by 20.17% over the past year, while the market has fallen by 2.89%. The growth is related to the company's high-quality financial results for 2024. I believe that stocks, even after such a large increase, have a high upside, especially after the April correction of the entire stock market, which can be used.

First, the multiplier company trades near its historical median values, without significant premiums or discounts.

At the same time, compared with peers in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S, EV/EBITDA multipliers, the company trades at significant discounts when compared by average values. Unilever looks undervalued relative to the average values among competitors: the company's P/E is 25.47x, which is lower than the industry average of 31.60x, P/S is 23% lower than the market average, and EV/EBITDA is already 60% lower at 13.44x.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 40%), target value of P/E, P/S and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights (estimated weights of 20%). In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume conservatively a 2.0% growth rate in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 5.27%. Since the company is based in the UK, British data on equity risk premium, country risk premium and risk-free rate were used to calculate the WACC. This WACC level is associated with a low beta of 60M at 0.22x. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. For a conservative estimate, I also added 0.8% as the United Kingdom county risk premium and 0.5% as specific risk premium to the WACC calculations. The current 10-Year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 20.91x, P/S is 2.23x, and EV/EBITDA is 12.78x (I took historical averages).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $63.87 per share, with a growth potential of 10.31% to the current market price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 13.97%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating. Wall Street analysts are slightly less conservative in their price forecasts, but the general view of the company is similar – the consensus expects a price of $68.13 with an upside of about 17.67%.

Key risks

Firstly, competition is intensifying from both sides: from the top — from heavyweights like P&G, Nestlé and L'Oréal, and from the bottom — from a rapidly growing wave of young niche brands that are not afraid to experiment and, importantly, act much faster. The second risk is the growth rate in key categories. In the developed markets, consumption is stagnating, and price sensitivity is increasing. Against this background, external risks are also increasing, from tariffs such as those announced by Trump in the spring of 2025 to tough new EU regulations on packaging and emissions. If the GAP 2030 strategy starts to stall and progress slows down, the market can easily reconsider its expectations, especially given the recent CEO change and shareholders' expectations for rapid changes. In such circumstances, even a small loss of share or a slow quarter can cost a company a drop in quotes. These stocks are no longer immune from disappointment.

Key growth drivers

One of the main drivers is the emerging markets (India, Indonesia and Brazil). They account for more than 58% of all revenue, and demographic and consumer growth is still being observed there. The second powerful driver is the GAP 2030 strategy, which is really rebuilding the business. Moving away from non-core brands, betting on the top 30 and focusing on 24 priority markets, strengthening digital, paying more attention to the premium segment — all this has already begun to produce results. By the end of 2024, the operating margin improved, and sales volumes began to grow. The categorical management structure, the emphasis on AI, and increasing the speed of processes all combine to increase efficiency. Plus, the company has such a thing in its pocket as a potential unlock value through the allocation of Ice Cream. And the active movement towards wellness and premium cosmetics is a story about new markets and margin growth. The growth potential in the next 12-24 months is quite real, the only question now is how clearly and quickly everything will be executed.

Conclusion

Thus, the financial condition of Unilever at the end of 2024 – beginning of 2025 looks stable. Revenue has returned to growth, EBITDA and margins have improved, debt is under control, CapEx is not out of bounds, and free cash flow covers all obligations. The new management strategy is aimed at optimizing costs, focusing on the most marginal products and restoring market share. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $63.87 per share, with a growth potential of 10.31% to the current market price. The total return, together with the dividends paid for 2025, may amount to about 13.97%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Amazon Sellers May Pass Tariff Costs To Consumers - CEO
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Amazon CEO says third-party sellers may pass costs on to customers. (0:15) Stocks sink, ignoring a cool but pre-tariff CPI report. (2:05) Goldman Sachs forced to quickly reverse recession call. (6:00)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) CEO Andy Jassy says the retailer’s third-party sellers may “pass costs on” related to President Donald Trump’s tariffs to customers.

Jassy said in a TV interview that Amazon was still evaluating the impact of the new tariff landscape, with China tariffs at 125% and a baseline of 10% for other nations.

“I understand why, I mean, depending on which country you’re in, you don’t have 50% extra margin that you can play with,” Jassy said, adding his company has made strategic inventory purchases and is moving to renegotiate certain purchase orders to mitigate the impact of tariffs.

Separately, in his annual letter to shareholders, Jassy said Amazon is aiming to be the "Earth’s most customer-centric company," while highlighting AI infrastructure and services that the company has introduced and is working on.

Jassy said that "generative AI is going to reinvent virtually every customer experience we know and enable altogether new ones about which we’ve only fantasized."

Jassy noted that if a company's customer experiences are not planning to use these intelligent models, their ability to get smarter with feedback and data, and future agentic capabilities, they will not be competitive.

"How soon? It won’t all happen in a year or two, but, it won’t take ten either. It’s moving faster than almost anything technology has ever seen," he said.

The CEO noted that "substantial capital investment" is required for AI.

In Amazon Web Services, the faster demand grows, the more datacenters, chips, and hardware the company needs to procure. He added that the company spends this capital upfront.

In addition, Jassy talked about Project Kuiper, the company's low Earth orbit satellite network, which would help millions of people without broadband connectivity access internet.

He said the company is launching its first production satellites and will ultimately have over 3,200 in orbit over the next few years.

"While capital-intensive to launch, we believe Kuiper will be a meaningful operating income and ROIC business for us," he said.

Looking to today’s trading, normally the latest consumer inflation figures out before the bell (spoiler alert: they were cool) would be driving direction. But these days are anything but normal.

A day after what some are calling Blink Wednesday; stocks are pulling back. That’s not surprising given the breakneck rally seen in just a couple of hours in the previous session.

The S&P (SPY), Nasdaq (QQQ) and Dow (DIA) are in the red.

UBS says although the risk of a severe market downturn, or left tail scenario, has diminished, concerns still linger.

“We believe even these reduced tariffs will imply a serious hit to growth. We expect negative earnings revisions to continue and create a headwind for the market. We don’t expect the highs to be tested and would recommend selling rallies until we learn more,” strategists said.

In the bond market, which would be doing victory laps today if markets could do laps, yields are coming down. But the 10-year (US10Y) is seeing support at 4.3%.

Skyler Weinand, chief investment officer of Regan Capital, says: “The bond market has been acting out of sync since the Fed started cutting rates in September 2024.”

“Many participants are hoping for lower rates to alleviate some price pressures on borrowers, but at the same time, inflation is out of control and hurting Main Street. Inflation, combined with a lack of buyers for U.S. Treasuries, as well as some leveraged payers becoming forced sellers over the last few days, have created much higher treasury yields over the last week.”

Speaking of inflation, the March CPI figures would be getting all the plaudits were it not for the new tariff landscape.

The headline unexpectedly edged down -0.1% M/M compared with a +0.2% increase in February and the +0.1% consensus On a Y/Y basis, the inflation measure rose 2.4%, vs. the +2.6% consensus and +2.8% prior.

The month-over-month movement reflected a 6.3% drop in the index for gasoline, which more than offset increases in the indexes for electricity and natural gas. The food index rose 0.4% M/M, with the food at home index rising 0.5% and the food away from home index increasing 0.4% during the month.

Core CPI, which strips out food and energy prices, increased 0.1% M/M in March, also slower than the +0.3% consensus and +0.2% in February. That translates to +2.8% Y/Y vs. +3.0% consensus and +3.1% prior. It also marks the smallest 12-month increase since March 2021.

But as BlackRock’s Rick Rieder said, this is an inflation print before “things got interesting.”

Pantheon Macro Economist Samuel Tombs says tariffs will snatch defeat from the jaws of inflation victory.

“Looking ahead, we think core CPI inflation will peak later this year at 3.5%, with core PCE inflation at 3.2%, if the current menu of tariffs stays in place,” he said. “That forecast is based on an average effective tariff rate on US imports of 17%, entailing around a 1pp boost to the level of consumer prices.”

“While full pass through from tariffs to consumer prices would imply a bigger uplift of about 1.5pp, we think retailers will struggle to pass on all the costs.”

Among active stocks, CarMax (KMX) is tumbling as an 81% increase in the company’s profit fell short of Wall Street’s lofty expectations.

And DexCom (DXCM) is rallying after it said the FDA has cleared its Dexcom G7 15 Day continuous glucose monitoring system for people over the age of 18 with diabetes.

In other news of note, DoorDash (DASH) will begin offering sidewalk robot delivery in select U.S. cities through an expansion of its partnership with food delivery robotics company Coco Robotics.

The rollout between DoorDash and Coco Robotics is currently live in Los Angeles and Chicago, where DoorDash customers can have their orders delivered by a fleet of emissions-free sidewalk robots.

Harrison Shih, senior director of DoorDash Labs, says: “Not every delivery needs a 2-ton car just to deliver two chicken sandwiches. We believe the future of delivery will be multi-modal.”

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman Sachs was forced into a quick about-face as the president backed off from his global trade war.

Right before 1 PM ET on Wednesday, its economists wrote that they expected to change their baseline forecast for the U.S. economy to a recession. They bumped up their forecast for a recession in the next 12 months to 65%.

But after Trump’s big move, Goldman reverted to its non-recession baseline.

Chief economist Jan Hatzius said: “Together, these tariffs are likely to sum to something close to our previous expectation of a 15 percentage points increase in the effective tariff rate. As a result, we are reverting to our previous non-recession baseline forecast, with GDP growth of 0.5% and a 45% probability of recession.”

Mohamed El-Erian said: “The market roller coaster does not surprise me. Indeed, it is to be widely expected. This does surprise me. A recession call – on or off – should not be this volatile or this dependent on a single policy issue, even if it's tariffs.”

data.title: Pulse Biosciences: Some Key Questions Remain Unanswered
data.description:
Today, Pulse Biosciences, Inc. (NASDAQ:PLSE) goes under the microscope. While this medical device company has no revenues yet, it is following an interesting treatment approach and has intriguing technology. That has pushed the stock up roughly 100% over the past 12 months.

Pulse Biosciences, Inc. is headquartered a quarter of a mile from my old condo in Miami in the Brickell area.

The company is focused on delivering nanosecond-long pulses of electrical energy to non-thermally clear targeted cells. The goal is to spare adjacent non-cellular tissue to treat various medical conditions. Pulse does this through its CellFX System. This is a tunable, software-enabled, and console-based platform that delivers nanosecond-long pulses of electrical energy. Leadership believes its technology can replace thyroidectomies with a non-surgical treatment.

The company has had some recent turnover in the C-Suite. In January, Pulse named its Co-Chairman to the additional post of CEO. In early February, the firm hired a new CFO from outside the company. The company's technology has shown relevance in clinical studies in treating atrial fibrillation or AFib, sebaceous hyperplasia, cutaneous warts and other cardiac conditions. There was a quite granular article on Seeking Alpha in late December that went into detail about Pulse's progress to date. Management also provided an update on its progress in Surgical AF Ablation, Endocardial Catheter AF Ablation and Soft Tissue Ablation in the quarterly earnings press release in late March.

In the summer of last year, the company's CellFX nanosecond pulse field ablation cardiac surgery system received Breakthrough Therapy designation for treating AFib. In May of last year, Pulse's CellFX nsPFA Percutaneous Electrode System received 510(k) clearance from the FDA for use in the ablation of soft tissue in percutaneous and intraoperative surgical procedures.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Despite an approximate $1 billion market capitalization, Pulse Biosciences gets very scant attention from Wall Street. In fact, I cannot find one analyst firm rating on the stock over the past 12 months on TipRanks.

Just over three percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Pulse Biosciences ended FY2024 with $118 million in cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet after posting a net GAAP loss of $19.4 million. This was up from a net loss of $11.9 million in 4Q2023. Pulse posted a GAAP net loss of $53.6 million, up from $42.2 million in FY2023. Pulse used $36 million in cash to support all operational activities in FY2024, up from $32.8 million.

The company listed no long-term debt on the 10-K filed for FY2024. However, Pulse has been a frequent user of its At The Market or ATM facility to raise additional capital. This facility was established in early 2021. The company also raised approximately $50 million via private placement in July 2021; Pulse garnered some $15 million via a rights offering in June 2022; and in May 2023, it got approximately $65 million via stock placement with the company's Chairman, Robert Duggan. That brought his ownership of Pulse Biosciences to nearly 70%. In the spring of 2023, Pulse got another $15 million from the exercise of warrants attached to a rights offering in 2022. In the spring of 2024, the firm got some $60 million from another rights offering. Most of that offering was purchased by the Chairman of the company and its majority shareholder. Finally, in the final quarter of 2024, it raised nearly $48 million from the exercise of warrants from the July 2024 rights offering.

Management Turnover:

Pulse Biosciences has had its share of management turnover in recent years in addition to the recently announced ones previously covered. The company appointed a Chief Strategy Officer in 2021. In September 2022, Pulse made some other leadership changes, including its CEO. The firm's CFO stepped down in late 2022. The company appointed a new Chief Medical Officer in May 2023. Pulse Biosciences made a couple of other management changes, including the CEO, in May 2024.

Conclusion:

Pulse Biosciences has some obvious caveats around any investment thesis. The company has no coverage on Wall Street despite a $1 billion market capitalization, it has diluted shareholders via the use of its ATM facility, rights offerings and exercises of warrants. It should be noted that the Chairman of the company has continued to buy most of these to maintain his ownership position. The company has seen consistent management changes in recent years as well.

The articles written here on Seeking Alpha go into the potential of the company's technology and that Pulse is targeting large potential end markets. However, there doesn't seem to be any timeline on when Pulse Biosciences will start generating significant commercialized revenues.

And this is not a knock in any way on those that have tried to present the investment case around Pulse Biosciences. If one goes through the company's last quarterly earnings conference call transcript, it is also big on the potential of Pulse's technology and the markets it is targeting. The transcript touches briefly on the numerous clinical trials and feasibility studies going on at numerous sites.

What doesn't seem to ever happen is any outline around when potential commercialization that will bring in revenues will begin. And at this point, the company has been public for nine years now. In fact, while the conference call does ask for questions from analysts, no questions from the analyst community come forth. I have gone through thousands of conference call transcripts over the past 15 years or so. I can't ever remember that happening. The same thing occurred on the company's Q3 earnings call transcript. Which begs the question: who is attending these conference calls.

The company consistently highlights its proprietary technology, clinical potential and significant market opportunities. However, until I can model out some sort of potential revenue timeline for Pulse, the stock is an avoid.

data.title: Quarterly Reviews For Dividend Investors: Your Key To Portfolio Peace Of Mind
data.description:
Let’s face it - most dividend investors don’t wake up excited to dig into earnings reports. It’s not exactly sipping-coffee-on-the-deck kind of fun. But reviewing your holdings quarterly? That’s not busywork - it’s your best defense against dividend surprises, sleepless nights, and “why didn’t I sell this earlier?” regrets.

At Dividend Stocks Rock (DSR), we don’t believe in watching the market daily. That’s a surefire path to analysis paralysis (and probably some unnecessary trades). But checking in four times a year? That’s the sweet spot. You stay informed, catch red flags early, and—most importantly—reinforce your confidence when things are going well.

In this post, we will walk you through:

  • Why quarterly reviews matter (even for long-term buy-and-hold investors),

  • What to look for in earnings reports,

  • And how to decide if your stock deserves a spot in your portfolio for another quarter—or not.

It’s all about making your dividend strategy stronger, not more complicated.

Let’s start with a simple truth: reviewing your portfolio isn’t about second-guessing your decisions—it’s about validating your convictions.

Why You Need to Review Your Stocks Quarterly (and How to Start Without Overwhelm)

Confidence Is the Real Dividend

For dividend growth investors, seeing a consistent income stream is great—but knowing your holdings are solid? That’s where real peace of mind comes from. A quarterly review isn’t about micromanaging your portfolio—it’s about reaffirming your decisions (or catching red flags early).

“Reviewing quarterly results isn’t thrilling—but it’s way more fun than watching a dividend cut unfold.”

Why It Matters

  • Spot issues before the dividend is in danger.

  • Reassure yourself that your investment thesis still holds.

  • Stay in control instead of reacting to market noise.

Where to Find the Right Info

Make the company’s Investor Relations (IR) section your best friend. Start with:

  • The press release of the quarterly results.

  • The quarterly presentation (usually includes graphs and visuals).

  • Conference call transcripts or replays.

  • The Management Discussion & Analysis (MD&A) provides more profound insights.

3 Simple Questions That Tell You Everything You Need to Know About a Stock’s Quarterly Results

1. Is the Company Growing Revenue, Profits, and Dividends?

This is the heartbeat of your stock. Growth across the board = healthy.

  • Compare YoY results: Q1’25 vs Q1’24.

  • Seasonal effects? Be mindful (e.g., winter coats won’t sell in June).

  • Sequential analysis: Sometimes Q1 ? Q2 is more revealing than Q1 ? Q1.

  • Check if the dividend grew vs last quarter or last year. Has it at least kept pace with inflation?

2. Is the Dividend Still Safe?

To ensure my dividends are safe, I start with the dividend triangle.

Normally, 5-year or 10-year growing trends are a good sign that a business is healthy.

A slowing dividend growth rate could be a warning sign—don’t ignore it.

Look at the payout ratio (dividend ÷ EPS). Is it rising uncomfortably over time?

In the DSR Stock Comparison tool below, you can clearly see how ScotiaBank (BNS:CA) is in a weaker state than its peers.

While the dividend remains safe for now, its payout ratio is higher, and its dividend growth is lower. These are two signs that your money may not be invested in the industry’s leader.

Pro Tip: Compare the company’s payout ratio against its sector norms using your DSR tools.

3. What’s the Future Outlook?

Even the best quarterly performance can be overshadowed by weak guidance.

What to watch:

  • Mergers and acquisitions: buying or merging with other companies brings challenges but also growth opportunities.
  • Divestitures: companies getting rid of non-productive assets can improve their future results.
  • Is the company launching new products or product categories?
  • Is the company expanding geographically or into related businesses?
  • CAPEX amounts: is the company investing a lot in its infrastructure? Will it likely bring revenue growth?
  • How much new debt is the company assuming? Is it repaying some of its debt?
  • Is the company buying back shares or renewing their share repurchase authorization?
    • When companies buy back shares, they reduce the number of outstanding shares in the hands of shareholders, which can increase earnings per share. Conversely, companies issuing more shares to raise capital are diluting the EPS with more outstanding shares.
  • What is the company’s outlook for the full year, or the new year? Is it anticipating growth? Has it revised its earlier outlook for better or worse?
    • A company increasing its outlook for revenue or profit is confident about the near future, whereas one revising its outlook downwards might be facing challenges.
  • Is it implementing cost-cutting or efficiency programs to improve profitability?

Bullish signals: Management raises guidance or invests heavily in future growth.

Caution flags: Lowered guidance, mounting debt, or unexplained earnings drops.

Bonus: The Quarterly Review Checklist: DSR Style

  • Revenue ? year-over-year?

  • EPS ? year-over-year?

  • Dividend ? or held steady (and above inflation)?

  • Payout ratio under control?

  • Future guidance shows growth or stability?

  • No red flags in debt or business model?

“Your stocks don’t have to be perfect. They just have to keep getting better.”

Wondering which Stocks to Keep? Make Smarter Decisions

Stop second-guessing your decisions and start investing with clarity.

Our Stock Buy Checklist helps you cut through the noise, ask the right questions, and focus on what really matters—dividend safety, growth potential, and your portfolio’s goals.

Whether you’re vetting your next pick or doing a final gut-check, this tool brings structure to your process and conviction to your buys.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: QQQ: I Have Been Waiting For A Drop Like This
data.description:
Recent market turmoil, which heavily affected technology companies, has created a golden buying opportunity for the Invesco QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ), an ETF that has generated impressive total returns since its inception in 1999. With markets cratering on concerns about new tariffs, I believe investors with a long-term investment horizon have a unique opportunity to start a position in one of the most successful technology ETFs. With many tech companies now being for sale, investors face a rare doubling-down opportunity, and companies like Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN) or Nvidia (NVDA) will continue to dominate their respective industries and benefit from advancements in AI.

Previous rating

I rated the Invesco QQQ ETF a buy in my last work—QQQ: An Aggressive Bet On AI Adoption—as I liked the ETF’s heavy tech focus as well as the aggressive overweighting of the world’s largest and most promising technology companies. With AI adoption expected to rise long term, I believe the valuation drawdown creates a solid opportunity for investors to build AI/tech exposure at a much better valuation than compared to just a few weeks ago. Since the Invesco QQQ ETF has outperformed for a very long time, I believe it is one of the best ways to own America's most promising tech enterprises.

Portfolio composition has changed since my last coverage

The Invesco QQQ ETF has a very heavily concentrated investment portfolio with ‘magnificent 7’ stocks, which are the seven dominant big tech companies in the U.S. and in the world. The magnificent seven stocks include Google, Nvidia, Amazon, Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Tesla (TSLA) and Meta Platforms (META) all of which are included, to varying degrees, in the Invesco QQQ ETF’s portfolio.

The biggest position in QQQ's portfolio now is Apple with a representation of 8.8%, which is about the same weighting the tech firm had in October 2024 when I last reviewed the Invesco QQQ ETF. However, the second-largest position is no longer Nvidia but Microsoft, with a weighting of 8.3% (7.8% in October). Another change that occurred since my last coverage is that the ETF now includes Netflix (NFLX) as a top 10 holding. Since the Invesco QQQ ETF still owns Alphabet Class A common shares (not included in the top ten holdings) the ETF still has combined Google exposure of approximately 5.17%… making the technology company actually the fifth-largest holding.

A recent (March 2025) report from research firm Gartner highlighted just how aggressive companies are planning to ramp up their spending on AI. According to Gartner, firms are set to spend serious money on artificial intelligence products, mostly generative AI, this year.

The research company expects that companies will spend $643.9B on Gen-AI products, both software and hardware, in FY 2025, which implies a massive 76% year-over-year increase. The biggest year-over-year increase is expected to happen in the Services market (+162.6% growth), followed by Devices and Software. Since most of Invesco QQQ ETF's holdings are heavily focused on developing AI products, I believe the ETF is an excellent choice for investors to continue to play the AI theme in FY 2025 and beyond.

What I like specifically about the Invesco QQQ ETF is that

  • The portfolio is concentrated in large-cap tech stocks, all of which dominate their respective industries (Amazon dominates Cloud, Google dominates Search, Meta dominates social media advertising, Nvidia leads the AI GPU market...)
  • The focus on magnificent 7 stocks makes QQQ a high-quality, high-growth tech play that is focused on the leading tech enterprises in the U.S. stock market
  • Companies are expected to ramp up their spending on their AI products to boost profitability and efficiencies -- as per Gartner's report -- which should make the theme of AI a secular, not a cyclical one
  • With many major tech companies trading at P/E ratios below 20X -- on a FY 2026 earnings basis -- I expect investors to rotate more cash into the tech sector
  • Invesco QQQ ETF has historically outperformed other tech-heavy ETF choices and has generated serious alpha for investors long-term.

As to the last point, Invesco QQQ ETF generated a life-term total return of 10.0% annually. The ETF was incepted on March 10, 1999, and therefore the ETF’s performance record includes major market crashes, including the Dot-com bubble, the real estate and financial sector meltdown in 2007-8 and the Covid-19 crash. Despite these massive, decade-defining events, the Invesco QQQ ETF has always rebounded and delivered strong growth in its net asset value.

QQQ’s valuation vs. tech-heavy ETF rivals

Invesco QQQ ETF is valued based on its net asset value, which reflects the total value of its underlying equity investments, and the ETF has had consistent, long-term growth in its NAV since it was incepted more than 25 years ago. The Invesco QQQ ETF also significantly outperformed other tech-heavy ETF investments, including the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) or the Vanguard Information Technology Index Fund (VGT). In fact, QQQ has achieved considerable 'alpha' compared to both other ETFs, in large part because of its aggressive overweighting of companies like Nvidia.

QQQ is trading at a 0.06 premium to the ETF's net asset value, which is above the ETF's 3-year average premium, but because tech stocks have sold off so heavily in recently, I believe QQQ is an exceptional way to own the leading U.S. tech franchises.

This year, all magnificent 7 stocks are, in terms of performance, in negative territory. Nvidia has lost the most, with its P/E ratio contracting 44% so far this year. However, since AI trends are long-term in nature and not so cyclical, I believe the current consolidation in the (tech) market creates an exceptionally attractive buying opportunity for investors here.

Risks with QQQ

The biggest risk I see for the Invesco QQQ ETF relates to a prolonged valuation drawdown.
